perturbation.original_text: 5.5. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York. The parties unconditionally and irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of New York and waive any objection with respect thereto, for the purpose of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.2. Assignment. Licensee shall not sublicense, assign, pledge, grant or otherwise encumber or transfer to any third party all or any part of its rights or duties under this Agreement, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the Licensor, which consent Licensor may grant or withhold in its sole and absolute discretion. Any purported transfer without such consent shall be void ab initio.

5.5. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York. The parties unconditionally and irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of Delaware and waive any objection with respect thereto, for the purpose of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware in the assignment section will lead to enforcing the contract under Delaware law. This creates confusion as the contract was originally intended to be enforced under New York law. This placement is misleading as it is not in a dedicated 'Governing Law' section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the governing law provision and changing the jurisdiction creates ambiguity regarding venue. 28 U.S. Code § 1391 addresses the proper venue for civil actions, and contracts often specify governing law and consent to jurisdiction to avoid disputes over venue. The changed text creates uncertainty and potential conflict, as a court might consider the original intent and conduct of the parties when determining the proper venue, despite the altered contract language.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w

perturbation.justification: The change introduces a structural flaw by placing a governing law provision within the assignment section, potentially contradicting the original intent of governing the entire agreement under New York law as stated in section 5.5 of the original contract. This misplacement creates ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction applies to the contract as a whole. The LLM explanation points out that 28 U.S. Code § 1391 addresses venue for civil actions, and the altered text creates uncertainty, potentially leading to disputes over proper venue. The scraped law snippets, while addressing venue generally, highlight the importance of clearly defined jurisdiction to avoid such disputes, and the misplaced provision undermines this clarity.

perturbation.original_text: 5.11. Entire Agreement. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and undertakings, both written and oral, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text:
3.1. Licensee accepts this license on an "as is" basis. Licensee acknowledges that Licensor makes no explicit or implicit representation or warranty as to the registrability, validity, enforceability, ownership of the Licensed Mark, or as to Licensee's ability to use the Licensed Mark without infringing or otherwise violating the rights of others, and Licensor has no obligation to indemnify Licensee with respect to any claims arising from Licensee's use of the Licensed Mark, including without limitation any Third Party Claim.

5.11. Entire Agreement. This Agreement constitutes a partial agreement of the parties with respect to some of the subject matter hereof and does not supersede all prior agreements and undertakings, both written and oral, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the 'entire agreement' clause and changing it to a 'partial agreement' in the representations and warranties, it is now implied that there are external or other agreements that affect this contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the 'entire agreement' clause to 'partial agreement,' the contract now suggests that external agreements may exist. This conflicts with UCC § 2-202 (Parol Evidence Rule), which generally states that a final written agreement intended as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement cannot be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or contemporaneous oral agreement. By suggesting the agreement is partial, the changed text undermines the principle that the written contract represents the complete understanding, potentially allowing outside evidence to alter or contradict the written terms, creating contractual instability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the 'entire agreement' clause to a 'partial agreement,' suggesting external agreements exist. This contradicts UCC § 2-202, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a final written agreement intended as a complete statement of terms cannot be contradicted by prior agreements. The change undermines the principle that the written contract represents the complete understanding, potentially allowing outside evidence to alter or contradict the written terms.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2. Upon Termination. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, all rights granted to Licensee under this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Mark shall cease, and Licensee shall immediately delete the term "Palmer Square" from its corporate name and shall discontinue all other use of the Licensed Mark. For twenty-four (24) months following termination of this Agreement, Licensee shall specify on all public-facing materials in a prominent place and in prominent typeface that Licensee is no longer operating under the Licensed Mark, is no longer associated with Licensor, or such other notice as may be deemed necessary by Licensor in its sole discretion in its prosecution, defense, and/or settlement of any Third Party Claim.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.3. Independent Contractor. Neither party shall have, or shall represent that it has, any power, right or authority to bind the other party to any obligation or liability, or to assume or create any obligation or liability on behalf of the other party.

4.2. Upon Termination. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, all rights granted to Licensee under this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Mark shall cease, and Licensee shall continue to use the term "Palmer Square" from its corporate name and shall continue all other use of the Licensed Mark. For twenty-four (24) months following termination of this Agreement, Licensee may specify on all public-facing materials in a non-prominent place and in small typeface that Licensee is still operating under the Licensed Mark and is associated with Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the Termination clause into the Independent Contractor section and negating the restrictions, this is now hidden in a completely different context and is a contradiction. The licensee is now able to continue using the licensed mark.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the licensee can continue to use the trademark after the termination and placing it under 'independent contractor,' this now conflicts with trademark law. 15 U.S.C. § 1114 addresses trademark infringement, and it generally prohibits the use of a registered mark in a way that is likely to cause confusion, mistake, or deception. After termination, the licensee no longer has the right to use the Licensed Mark, and any continued use could infringe upon the Licensor's rights, potentially violating the Lanham Act. By making the clause non-prominent, this increases the likelihood of confusion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts trademark law, specifically 15 U.S.C. § 1114, which prohibits the use of a registered mark in a manner likely to cause confusion after the termination of a license agreement. The original text clearly states that upon termination, the licensee must cease using the licensed mark. However, the changed text allows the licensee to continue using the mark, and even specifies that they can indicate they are still operating under the licensed mark. This directly conflicts with the licensor's rights and could lead to consumer confusion, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that any unauthorized use of a registered mark that is likely to cause confusion is a violation, making the contradiction explicit and impactful.
